coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (Hg2+) in Oreochromis niloctus (Tilapia fish)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Aladesuyi, Olanrewaju Aladesanmi
Oluwafemi, Oluwatobi Samuel
coredata.dc:description: This study reported the synthesis of green-emitting N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) through a one-step, facile, and fast hydrothermal technique using citric acid, glutamine, and Na2S as the carbon, nitrogen, and sulphur sources respectively. The fluorescence efficiency of the developed nanosensor was investigated by varying the duration of synthesis (4, 5, and 6 h). Characterization of the N-CQDs was achieved using Fourier-Transform Infra-red Spectroscopy (FTIR), High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), and X-Ray diffraction analysis (XRD). The as-synthesized N,S-CQDs are small (average size of 3.45 ± 0.86 nm), spherical, and displayed green emission (535 nm) with a fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 10.35%. FTIR analysis reveals carboxylic, hydroxyl, and conjugated amide functional groups. The fluorometric study showed that the developed nanosensor was selective and sensitive towards Hg2+ ions in the midst and absence of interfering ions with a detection limit (LOD) of 28.9 nM. The static quenching mechanism was proposed based on lifetime and UV results. Furthermore, it was used as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ determination in Oreochromis niloctus with recoveries of 96.7–108.6% (RSD < 4.1%), indicating the as-synthesized N,S-CQDs have potentials as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ determination in sea foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Tilapia
Nanoprobe
Hg2+
Glutamine
Green emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of N,S-CQDs towards Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra, (b) FTIR spectra,(c) TEM micrograph (inset: size distribution and lattice fringes) and (d) XRD pattern of the N,S-CQDs.
(a) PL spectra of (a) N and N,S doped CQDs; (b) N,S-CQDs at varied reaction times (c) N,S-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ standards and (d) corresponding Stern-Volmer plot (R2 = 0.9944).
Selectivity evaluation of N,S-CQDs towards Hg2 + (a) and other metal ions (b) in the midst of interfering metal ions at a concentration of 50 μM.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of N,S-CQDs and N,S-CQDs/Hg2+(b) UV–Vis spectra of N,S-CQDs and N,S-CQDs/Hg2+.
Comparism of previously reported CQDs based nanoprobe for Hg2+ detection and present study.
Analysis of results in Oreochromis niloctus actual samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (Hg2+) in Oreochromis niloctus (Tilapia fish)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, P.O. Box 17011, Doornfontein 2028, Johannesburg, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Johannesburg, P.O. Box 17011, Doornfontein 2028, Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanomaterials Science Research, University of Johannesburg, Johannesburg, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanomaterials Science Research, University of Johannesburg, Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reported the synthesis of green-emitting N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) through a one-step, facile, and fast hydrothermal technique using citric acid, glutamine, and Na2S as the carbon, nitrogen, and sulphur sources respectively. The fluorescence efficiency of the developed nanosensor was investigated by varying the duration of synthesis (4, 5, and 6 h). Characterization of the N-CQDs was achieved using Fourier-Transform Infra-red Spectroscopy (FTIR), High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), and X-Ray diffraction analysis (XRD). The as-synthesized N,S-CQDs are small (average size of 3.45 ± 0.86 nm), spherical, and displayed green emission (535 nm) with a fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 10.35%. FTIR analysis reveals carboxylic, hydroxyl, and conjugated amide functional groups. The fluorometric study showed that the developed nanosensor was selective and sensitive towards Hg2+ ions in the midst and absence of interfering ions with a detection limit (LOD) of 28.9 nM. The static quenching mechanism was proposed based on lifetime and UV results. Furthermore, it was used as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ determination in Oreochromis niloctus with recoveries of 96.7–108.6% (RSD < 4.1%), indicating the as-synthesized N,S-CQDs have potentials as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ determination in sea foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury has been described as a global priority pollutant due to its detrimental impact on the environment and human health in general [1]. Mercury exposure can result in chronic ailments such as kidney disease, immune system disorder, heart disease, and some other conditions, even when exposure is minimal [2,3]. Industrial processes such as the electrolysis of brine and battery production require mercury, consequently contaminating water bodies in such areas. In water bodies, mercury can be found in its divalent form (Hg2+) or organic forms such as methyl, ethyl, and phenyl mercury [4]. Since the body mechanism cannot discard mercury, there exists a gradual accumulation of mercury in vital body organs on exposure, with the concentration increasing with time. Consumption of seafood such as fish is one of the significant ways humans are exposed to mercury [5].

Regarding this, adequately monitoring mercury levels in consumed seafood is critical in ensuring human health and safety. Conventional analytical procedures have been used for mercury detection. They include; Inductive coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [6], Atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) [7], electrochemical technique [8], and the like. However, these analytical procedures require the use of expensive equipment and also the use of trained personnel. Fluorescence nanosensors have recently gained a lot of attention in the detection of heavy metals. In this regard, nanosensors such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been preferred as selective metal sensors for quantification analysis due to their water solubility, low toxicity, resistance to photobleaching, etc. [9,10]. CQDs' fluorescence properties and sensing abilities can be improved through doping or co-doping with heteroatoms such as N, S, P, etc. [11–13]. The similarity in atomic size and electronegativity of carbon and the dopant ions are some of the reasons for the improved optical properties of the CQDs [14]. Although reports have shown the use of CQDs for Hg2+ sensing applications in actual samples [15,16]. For example, Liu et al. [17] developed an N,S-CQDs nanoprobe for the selective detection of Hg2+ using cysteine and polyethyleneimine. The obtained nanoprobe showed high sensitivity and selectivity to Hg2+ in actual samples of lake water and rice samples. Wu and Tong [18] synthesize N,S-CQDs hydrothermally using ammonium citrate and L-cysteine as precursors. The obtained nanosensor selectively detects Hg2+ in an aqueous solution with a limit of detection LOD of 83 nM. However, detecting Hg2+ in actual seafood samples has rarely been reported.

Herein, we reported the hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-CQDs using Citric acid (CA), glutamine, and Na2S as the carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources. The synthetic condition was optimized concerning reaction time (3, 4, and 5 h). N, S co-doping enhances the fluorescent intensity, quantum yield, and sensing performance of the carbon quantum dots compared to the singly N-doped. The performance of the developed nanosensor in detecting Hg2+ in the presence and absence of other interfering ions was investigated. The N,S-CQDs selectively detect Hg2+ amidst other metal ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 28.9 nM. We studied the quenching mechanism using fluorescence lifetime and UV–Vis spectroscopy. Furthermore, the as-synthesized N, S-CQDs were utilized as a nanoprobe in determining the amount of Hg2+ in the tilapia fish sample. Satisfactory recoveries of 96.7–108.6% were obtained.

CA, glutamine, NaOH, H2SO4, n-butanol, HgCl2, Cr(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Tilapia fish were purchased from Shoprite supermarket in Johannesburg. All materials were used as purchased without any further purification.

Briefly, 6 mmoles of CA, 9 mmoles of glutamine, and 9mmole of Na2S were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water under slight stirring until a homogenous mixture was formed. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling, the yellowish solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm. Afterward, the supernatant was washed with n-butanol several times to remove dissolved impurities. The solution was filtered in a 0.02 µm filter paper and freeze-dried to give solid crystals. The experiment was repeated for 4 and 5 h to investigate the effect of reaction time on the luminescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of N,S-CQDs towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL of aqueous N,S-CQDs was added to separate cuvettes containing 3 mL of various concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0–70 μΜ). The fluorescent spectra of the mixtures were measured and recorded at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm after 5 mins of incubation to allow proper interaction between the N, S-CQDs and the Hg2+ ions. The same procedure was repeated using metal ion salts of Fe(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, NaCl and KCl. For selectivity studies, same procedure was carried out using a mixture equal volume of Hg2+ with the other interfering ions.

For real sample analysis, 1 g of fish tissue was placed in a flask containing 1:1 HNO3. The mixture was heated for about 30 min with continuous addition of the acid and H2O2 until digestion was complete. The resultant solution was filtered with a filter paper, transferred into a 100 mL standard flask, and diluted to the mark using deionized water. The pH was adjusted to 6 using 1 M NaOH. To detect Hg2+ ions, 3 mL of the solution was placed in a cuvette containing 100 μL of aqueous N,S-CQDs and the photoluminescence spectra were recorded at 450 nm.

The morphology and particle size were obtained with a JOEL (JEM 2100) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A Spectrum Two Perkin Elmer Fourier transforms infrared instrument (FTIR) fitted with an attenuated total reflection sample holder with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 was used to investigate the surface chemistry. The fluorescence spectrum, lifetime and quantum yield (QY) were obtained with an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburg instrument). The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were monitored in 1-cm quartz cuvettes using the double beam SP-UV 500 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using Brucker D8 Advance diffractometer with monochromatic CuKα1 radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å).

The UV absorption spectra (Fig. 1 a) exhibited two absorption peaks at 225 and 330 nm. These peaks are attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond and the n- π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b), peak at 3214 cm−1 correspond to the presence of O–H stretching vibration bands, indicating the presence of the carboxylic group in the N,S-CQDs. The peak at 3096 cm−1 shows the presence of the N–H stretching band from the conjugated amide group. The absorption band at 1661 and 1576 cm−1 represent CdbndO and C-NH groups. Also, the peak at 713 cm−1 was ascribed to C-S stretching vibrations [19], confirming the incorporation of sulphur. The peaks at 1406–1102 cm−1 are attributed to C–C, C-N, and C-O stretching vibration bands. The HRTEM micrograph (Fig. 1c) shows that the as-synthesized nanomaterials are spherical and uniformly dispersed. The size distribution (inset) shows an average size of 3.45 ± 0.86 nm. The presence of lattice fringes (Fig. 1c inset) affirms its crystallinity with the measured lattice spacing (0.23 nm) aligning with (002) lattice planes of graphite. XRD pattern showed a broad hump at 2ϴ = 21.55° (Fig. 1d) corresponding to the 002 graphite lattice plane, which agrees with the HRTEM result. This indicates the presence of a low graphitic carbon structure [20].

Fig. 2 a shows the PL spectra of the co-doped and the singly-doped CQDs when excited at 450 nm. The N, S co-doped CQDs displayed a higher fluorescent intensity than the N singly-doped. The emission peak position also red-shifted from 525 −535 nm. This can be attributed to the ability of the sulphur to absorb light at a lower energy and emit with a longer wavelength due to its low electronegativity [21]. The fluorescent intensity increases with reaction time, with a maximum at 4 h. This can be due to the increased rate of reaction and carbonization, as well as sulphur passivating the surface defect [22,23]. The decline in intensity beyond 4 h can result from impurity formation caused by excess carbonization destroying the core (Fig. 2b) [24]. Based on this, the reaction time of 4 h was used for further studies. The QY of the N, S co-doped CQDs and N-singly doped is 10.35% and 1.59%, respectively, indicating that the heteroatom co-doping improved the fluorescence efficiency and QY.

Under optimized synthetic conditions (200 °C, 4 hand pH 6), the effect of Hg2+ on the fluorescent intensity of the N, S-CQDs was studied. A gradual quenching of the fluorescent intensity was observed in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 2c). The fluorescent quenching can be due to the formation of a non-luminescent coordination complex between Hg2+ and the functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) present [25]. The linear relationship between different Hg2+ concentrations (Fig. 2d) in the range of 0.05–20 µM obeys the Stern-Volmer equation.
Fo
F
=
K
s v
C
+
1
Ksv -Stern -Volmer constant, C-concentration of Hg2+, Fo, and F is the PL intensities of N,S-CQD in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively.

The LOD (28.9 nM) was obtained using the formula LOD =
3 S D
S
Where SD is the standard deviation of the blank N,S-CQDs and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The obtained LOD is satisfactory compared to those previously reported in literature (Table 1 ), even though lower limit of detection for Hg2+ has been reported. For instance, Dewangan et al. [26], reported a LOD of 1.43 nM, 0.93 nM, and 1.38 nM using blue-emitting N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs, and N/Ce-CQDs nanoprobes, respectively. However, preparing CQDs emitting in the visible region is highly desirable for extensive applications. In addition, doping or co-doping of CQDs with metal atoms has been associated with increased toxicity [27].

The selectivity study (Fig. 3 a) shows that the N-S-CQDs is very selective toward Hg2+ compared to other metal ions. In addition, a mixture of Hg2+ ions with other interfering ions was added to an aqueous solution containing N,S-CQDs. The result (Fig. 3b) indicates that the quenching experienced was due to the effect of Hg2+ on the N,S-CQDs. This shows that the developed nanosensor exhibits a high selectivity for Hg2+ detection.

The fluorescent lifetime values and absorption spectra are essential in determining the quenching mechanism of CQDs [33]. To understand the quenching mechanism fully, the fluorescence lifetime and the UV spectra of the nanoprobe were recorded with and without the quencher ion (Hg2+). The obtained lifetime values were 3.55 ns and 3.46 ns in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively (Fig. 4 a). Furthermore, the absorption peak at 330 nm for N,S-CQDs/Hg2+ was less visible as compared to that of N,S-CQDs (Fig. 4b). This can be due to the complex formation between the nanosensor and Hg2+ indicating static quenching mechanism.

To ascertain the feasibility of the nanoprobe in actual samples, the nanoprobe was used to detect Hg2+ in Oreochromis niloctus. Hg2+ standards were added to treated samples of Oreochromis niloctus (Table 2 ) in the presence of the nanosensor, the PL spectra were measured and recorded. The actual spiked samples agree with the amount recovered (96.9%-108.6%) with a low relative standard deviation (1.44% −4.05%). These results affirm the reliability and accuracy of the developed nanosensor.

Herein, we reported the hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-CQDs as a nanosensor using CA, glutamine, and Na2S as precursors. The fluorescence efficiency of the as-synthesized N,S-CQDs was investigated by varying the reaction time of the precursors. The optimum synthetic time was 4 h. The as-synthesized N, S-CQDs are spherical with an average size of 3.45 ± 0.86 nm with a peak emission position at 535 nm when excited at 450 nm. FTIR confirms the presence of amine, carboxyl, and amine functional groups. The developed nanosensor was highly selective for determining Hg2+ in aqueous samples under optimized pH conditions with a LOD of 28.9 nM. The quenching mechanism investigated by fluorescence decay lifetime and UV–Vis spectrum of the N,S-CQDs, reveals static quenching. In addition, the N,S-CQDs showed high precision and reliability as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ determination in Oreochromis niloctus. This indicates its potential as a nanosensor for Hg2+ determination in sea foods.

Olanrewaju Aladesanmi Aladesuyi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Oluwatobi Samuel Oluwafemi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Research Foundation (N.R.F) under Competitive Programme for Rated Researchers (CPRR), Grants No. 129290, the University of Johannesburg research committee (URC) and the Faculty of Science research committee (FRC) for financial support. OAA also thanks Covenant University, Nigeria, for allowing him to take up a post-doctoral fellowship at the Department of Chemical Sciences, NANOBEW research group, University of Johannesburg, under the supervision of Prof. S.O. Oluwafemi.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Dong, M. Wang, Y. Tang, Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanospheres from chrysanthemum as a multifunctional sensor for permanganate, Hg(II), and captopril, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy 271 (2022) 120886.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped, silver, and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots based sensor for detection of Hg2+ and captopril
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dewangan, Y. Chawre, J.Korram, I. Karbhal, R. Nagwanshi, V. Jain, M.L. Satnami, N-doped, silver, and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots based sensor for detection of Hg2+ and captopril, Microchemical Journal 182 (2022) 107867.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Xu, T. Kuang, Y.Liu, L. Cai, X. Peng, T.S. Sreeprasad, P. Zhao, Z. Yue, N. Li, Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications, Journal of Material Chemistry B 4 (2016) 7204

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Easily synthesized carbon dots for determination of mercury (II) in water samples
Heliyon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Yahyazadeh, F. Shemirani, Easily synthesized carbon dots for determination of mercury (II) in water samples, Heliyon 5 (2019), p. e01596.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ Ions by Using High Fluorescent Carbon Dots Doped With S And N as Fluorescence Probes
J. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, H. Xu, H. Li. Detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ Ions by Using High Fluorescent Carbon Dots Doped With S And N as Fluorescence Probes, Journal of Fluorescence 32(3) (2022) 1089-1098.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The on–off-on Fluorescence Sensor of Hollow Carbon Dots for Detecting Hg2+ and Ascorbic Acid
J. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hao, R, Li, Y. Liu, X. Zhang, L. Geng, S. Chen, The on–off-on Fluorescence Sensor of Hollow Carbon Dots for Detecting Hg2+ and Ascorbic Acid, Journal of Fluorescence 33 (2023) 459–469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel Paper-Based Fluorescent Sensor Based on N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) and Cotton Fiber Paper (CFP) with High Selectivity and Sensitivity for the Visual Determination of Mercury (II) in Environmental Waters
Anal. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, Y. Yin, C. Sun, C. Ma, Novel Paper-Based Fluorescent Sensor Based on N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) and Cotton Fiber Paper (CFP) with High Selectivity and Sensitivity for the Visual Determination of Mercury (II) in Environmental Waters, Analytical Letters 56 (2023) 1065-1080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles embedded sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots: A fluorescent nanosensor for detection of mercury ions in aqueous media
Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Pattnayak, U. Sahoo, S. Choudhury, G. Hota, Silver nanoparticles embedded sulfur doped graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots: A fluorescent nanosensor for detection of mercury ions in aqueous media, Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects 648 (2022) 129377.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of mango ginger (Curcuma amada) derived fluorescent carbon dots—a potent label-free probe for hexavalent chromium sensing in water
Spectrosc. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Venugopalan, N. Vidya, Green synthesis of mango ginger (Curcuma amada) derived fluorescent carbon dots—a potent label-free probe for hexavalent chromium sensing in water, Spectroscopy Letters 55 (2022) 373-388.
